data.title: Delinquencies, charge-offs drop as consumers pull back on borrowing: March Credit Pulse
data.description:
U.S. credit card delinquencies and charge-offs dropped month-over-month across major banks in the prior month, according to Seeking Alpha's March Credit Pulse.

Seeking Alpha tracks delinquency and charge-off numbers for seven banks in its monthly roundup: American Express (AXP), Synchrony Financial (SYF), Citigroup (C), Bread Financial (BFH), Capital One (COF), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), and Bank of America (BAC).

Average delinquency rate dropped to 2.70% from 2.81% in February. Furthermore, the metric was below the March 2025 average of 2.84%.

Notably, the March delinquency rate came below the pre-pandemic level of 2.76% (March 2019) and the three-month average of 2.77%.

Average net charge-off rate for March came in at 3.38%, down from 3.83% in February and 4.34% in March 2025. The pre-pandemic rate was 4.05%, while the three-month average was 3.58%.

Households are pulling back on borrowing and reducing revolving balances, leading to improvements in consumer credit conditions, showed the latest CreditGauge edition from VantageScore.

Average credit card balances and utilization rates declined month-over-month in March, according to the credit scoring company.

"The decline in revolving balances provides clearer evidence of consumer deleveraging, suggesting households are paying down debt and moderating spending, supported in part by seasonal inflows such as tax refunds," said the report.

Here is a look at the March delinquencies and net charge-off rates:

data.title: DraftKings and Flutter see ratings cuts at MoffettNathanson due to prediction market overhang
data.description:
DraftKings (DKNG) and Flutter Entertainment (FLUT) were cut at MoffettNathanson to a Neutral rating from Buy.

While the firm has a core belief that the valuation of both companies is attractive, even on conservative longer-term forecasts, a major overhang is seen on both sports betting stocks  until there is some regulatory clarity on the prediction markets industry.

Currently, the U.S. prediction market is sitting in a rapidly evolving but still unsettled regulatory environment. The CFTC has moved toward a formal framework, including a March 12 advance notice of proposed rulemaking and a staff advisory, while also withdrawing an earlier proposal that could have tightened limits on sports- and politics-related contracts.

At the same time, state regulators and litigants are aggressively challenging the industry, especially where contracts overlap with gambling or sports betting. The result today is  a patchwork of federal oversight, active rulemaking, and ongoing state-level legal pressure.

DraftKings (DKNG) and Flutter Entertainment (FLUT) are both down more than 30% since the beginning of the year as prediction market trading volume continues to exceed expectations for major sporting events.

data.title: SLB rises following in-line Q1; to acquire S&P Global’s subsurface oil software business
data.description:
SLB (SLB) up 1.9% in early trading Friday after reporting Q1 adjusted earnings that matched Wall Street expectations but fell from a year ago, as widespread disruptions across the Middle East hurt its business.

The fallout from the war has been difficult for SLB (SLB) and other oilfield services contractors that have depended on the Middle East for growth as U.S. shale fields mature; the Middle East and Asia comprised nearly 35% of SLB's revenue in 2025.

Q1 profit fell to $752M, or $0.50/share, from $797M, or $0.58/share, in the year-earlier quarter, while revenue ticked up 2.7% Y/Y to $8.72B, as international revenue fell 3.8% to $6.47B while North American revenue jumped 26% to $2.17B.

Q1 revenue from the Middle East and ​Asia fell 10% to $2.69B, hurt by disruptions in Qatar ⁠due to a force majeure and in Iraq and offshore operations across ​the region from the war.

"It was a challenging start to the year as widespread disruptions in the Middle East impacted our business," SLB (SLB) CEO Olivier Le Peuch said. "The impact was most pronounced in Well Construction and Reservoir Performance, as SLB demobilized operations in a number of countries in response to customer actions to safeguard personnel and facilities."

The company expects crude prices to remain above pre-war levels even after the conflict ends, as damaged infrastructure, hobbled production, and increased risks for operators in the region drive up costs, Le Peuch said.

The CEO also said he expects increased investment in ⁠short-cycle ​projects in North America and Latin America as ​well as long-cycle developments once the war subsides.

SLB (SLB) also said it agreed to acquire S&P Global Energy's (SPGI) geoscience and petroleum engineering software business; financial terms were not disclosed.

SLB (SLB) expects the acquisition to complement its in-house technology used to analyze and model subterranean oil and natural gas deposits, and will make SLB more competitive in the U.S. shale market, where technology is especially crucial.

TD Cowen analyst Marc Bianchi, who rates SLB (SLB) as a Buy, noted Q1 EPS was in-line with the company's pre-announced $0.06-$0.09 impact from the war, and "while commentary is bullish around the eventual macro implications from the war (stronger oil/OFS market), the release stops short of providing any near-term outlook, noting 'uncertainties remain.'"

data.title: Anthropic highlights election safeguards on Claude ahead of US midterms
data.description:
Anthropic (ANTHRO) ​highlighted some safeguard measures it is taking ahead of the U.S. midterms as people turn to its AI chatbot Claude for information about political parties and candidates.

The company said it trains Claude to treat different political viewpoints with equal depth and engagement.

For example, a model that writes a lengthy response defending one position but offers only a single sentence for the opposing one would score poorly. Here, Anthropic said its models Opus 4.7 and Sonnet 4.6, scored 95% and 96%, respectively.

Anthropic noted that Claude can't be used to run deceptive political campaigns, create fake digital content to influence political discourse, commit voter fraud, interfere with voting systems, or spread misleading information about voting processes.

To measure how well Claude handles election related risks, the company has run a series of tests examining its responses to questions. The latest tests use 600 prompts to assess how well Claude follows the company's election-related Usage Policy, based on how people actually talk to Claude about elections. 

They consist of 300 harmful requests (such as attempts to have Claude generate election misinformation) paired with 300 legitimate requests (like creating campaign content or civic engagement resources).

Anthropic said Claude Opus 4.7 and Claude Sonnet 4.6 responded appropriately 100% and 99.8% of the time, respectively.

The company also tested how well Claude holds up against influence operations: coordinated efforts to manipulate public opinion or political outcomes through fake personas and fabricated content. In the latest evaluations, Sonnet 4.6 and Opus 4.7 both responded appropriately 90% and 94% of the time, according to the company.

In addition, Anthropic said that ahead of launching Mythos Preview — its most powerful AI model yet — and Opus 4.7, they tested for the first time if models can carry out influence operations autonomously — planning and running a multi-step campaign end-to-end without human prompting.

"With safeguards and training in place, our latest models refused nearly every task. Without our safeguards in place (which we do to measure a model's raw capabilities), only Mythos Preview and Opus 4.7 completed more than half the tasks," said Anthropic in the blog post on Friday.

The company also said that when users ask about voter registration, polling locations, election dates, or ballot information on Claude.ai, Claude surfaces an election banner pointing them to trusted sources. In this year’s U.S. midterm elections, the banner will direct users to TurboVote, a nonpartisan resource from Democracy Works that provides reliable, real-time information about those topics.

The company will implement a similar banner for Brazil’s elections later this year and will look to expand this feature to elections elsewhere in the future.

Anthropic added that this year, they ran evaluations on its models to see if web search was triggered when Claude was asked questions related to elections around the world.

For the US midterms, the company used over 200 distinct prompts, each with three variations (for a total of over 600). The prompts covered questions including: Who are the candidates running in the 2026 US midterm elections? Can you tell me which candidates have officially filed to run in the 2026 midterms? and what does the current field of 2026 midterm candidates look like?

Opus 4.7 and Sonnet 4.6 triggered web search on these types of questions 92% and 95% of the time, respectively, according to the company.

Anthropic noted that it is working with The Future of Free Speech (an independent think tank at Vanderbilt University), the Foundation for American Innovation, and the Collective Intelligence Project on a broader review of model behaviors around freedom of expression, including political conversations.

Earlier this month, Anthropic unveiled its most powerful model, Claude Mythos Preview, a general-purpose frontier model, which it called its "most capable yet for coding and agentic tasks." The company — which is backed by Amazon (AMZN) and Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL) Google — has discussed Mythos with the Trump administration despite a legal tussle between the two after a blacklisting.

Earlier this week, U.S. President Donald Trump said his administration expects to maintain a constructive relationship with Anthropic. Reportedly, the Trump Administration is looking to give federal agencies access to Mythos. 

The company, reportedly, is in “active talks” with the U.K. government about providing access to Mythos to several British businesses. Anthropic ​is also in discussion ‌with the European Commission for its different models, ​including the cyber​security ones.

Anthropic has made the model available in preview to about 50 companies, according to a report.

data.title: The U.S. is still ‘the cleanest shirt in the dirty laundry’ – strategist
data.description:
Markets have swung from extreme pessimism to excessive optimism in one of the quickest rebounds to new highs on record, according to Emily Roland, co-chief investment strategist at Manulife John Hancock Investment Management.

Roland warned that valuations are once again extended, and investors should be cautious about the current momentum-driven environment.

“It looks like all you need to do is really add the word ‘AI’ to your name in order to see stock price gains here,” she said, noting that lower-quality stocks are being rewarded in this climate.

Roland shared these observations during a recent interview with CNBC, invoking the old adage that “the stock market is not the economy.”

She pointed to weakening economic indicators, including declining readings on the Citi Economic Surprise Index in both the U.S. and Eurozone, contrasting sharply with booming market returns.

The U.S. remains “the cleanest shirt in the dirty laundry” with a PMI reading of 52, while the Eurozone’s composite PMI came in at a contractionary 48.6.

Despite the broader economic softness, the U.S. is experiencing what Roland described as a “sugar rush” from several fiscal tailwinds.

Increased capital expenditure spending from major legislation, tax refunds totaling $26B so far this year, and new tariff refunds are all providing temporary support. However, she emphasized that economic data is broadly weakening beneath these surface-level boosts.

Roland highlighted an unusual dynamic between U.S. and European markets, noting that U.S. earnings growth is approaching 30% compared to just 9% overseas, yet European stocks are outperforming.

“It’s almost like we need to earn every bit of appreciation from investors, and European stocks (VGK), (EZU), (BBEU), (IEUR) are getting a participation prize right now,” she said, suggesting this creates an opportunity to trim overseas holdings and redeploy capital into the U.S.

John Hancock remains overweight in U.S. technology stocks (VGT), (XLK), (IYW), (FTEC) because these companies offer “the best earnings growth prospects of any companies around the world,” Roland explained.

However, she acknowledged the sector is “overloved” and “overbought,” cautioning investors against “going over our skis here and taking risks” at current valuation levels.

For investors seeking opportunities beyond large-cap tech, Roland pointed to mid-cap stocks (IJH), (VO), (IWR), (MDY), (VOE) trading at reasonable valuations along with utilities (XLU), (VPU), (FUTY), (IDU), industrials (XLI), (VIS), (FXR), (IYJ), and infrastructure-related equities.

These sectors benefit from onshoring activity and “the picks and shovels around AI, data centers, and power demand,” she noted, offering attractive earnings prospects tied to the ongoing capital expenditure boom.

U.S. markets tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

data.title: DOJ set to drop Powell probe as Fed watchdog takes over cost overrun review
data.description:
The U.S. Department of Justice is expected to drop its criminal investigation into Jerome Powell as soon as Friday, shifting oversight of the matter to an internal watchdog review at the Federal Reserve.

In a statement posted on X, U.S. Attorney Jeanine Pirro said the Inspector General for the Federal Reserve has been asked to examine “billions of dollars” in cost overruns tied to building projects borne by taxpayers. She added that her office would close its probe while the IG conducts a “comprehensive” inquiry.

The move signals a de-escalation of legal pressure on Powell, who had faced scrutiny over the central bank’s spending controls. Pirro emphasized that the IG has the authority to hold the Fed accountable and expressed confidence that the review would address outstanding concerns that prompted subpoenas.

Still, she left the door open for renewed legal action, warning that a criminal investigation could be restarted “should the facts warrant doing so.”

The news comes after President Donald Trump, who has been urged by Republicans to drop U.S. prosecutors' investigation into Powell, indicated he may be open to an alternate probe, according to a media report on Friday.

The investigation centers on major cost overruns at the Fed's multibillion-dollar renovation and whether Powell lied to Congress about the project. Several Republican senators have stated that his testimony didn't constitute a crime. Powell and many others see the investigation as a pretext to pressure the Fed to lower rates.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: American Express in pact to embed Resy in Anthropic's Claude
data.description:
A partnership between American Express (AXP)  and Anthropic will allow Claude users to book restaurant reservations through Amex's Resy platform directly in the AI model, the companies said on Friday.

This partnership reflects American Express’s (AXP) focus on creating AI-powered experiences for card members and embedding its assets, like Resy, into leading AI platforms.

When a Claude user asks where to eat, the model will produce a list of relevant recommendations. The user can then connect to the Resy app within Claude, where they can then navigate to Resy-owned venue pages for more information and view available tables, American Express said.

data.title: Alpine Income Property Trust signals 2026 investment volume of $170M-$200M while raising FFO outlook to $2.09-$2.13
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alpine Income Property Trust (PINE) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We are pleased to report a strong first quarter in 2026, building on a record level of investment activity we achieved in 2025," said President, CEO & Director John Albright. "We continue to execute our investment strategy by seeking to assemble a high-quality portfolio of single-tenant net lease properties leased to investment-grade rated tenants in addition to originating commercial loans with attractive risk-adjusted returns..."

  • "During the quarter, we acquired a retail property in downtown Aspen, Colorado, for $10 million," said President, CEO & Director Albright. "This acquisition was structured as a 50-year absolute triple-net master lease and initial cap rate of 8.5% with 1.25% annual rent escalators." He also said the company "continue[s] to selectively prune" the portfolio, "selling 3 non-investment-grade-rated lease properties for $5.8 million."

  • "As a result of our combined first quarter property transactions, our property portfolio consists of 125 properties...with a 99.5% occupancy and a WALT of 9.3 years," said President, CEO & Director Albright, adding that "50% of our ABR is generated from investment-grade rated tenants."

  • "During the quarter, we originated a $32 million first mortgage loan, of which $8.6 million was funded at close," said President, CEO & Director Albright. "The loan carries a 24-month term with an initial interest rate of 13% inclusive of a 1.5% paid-in-kind interest, stepping down to an 11.5% current pay rate upon the borrower meeting certain conditions."

  • "We closed and funded the $31.8 million Phase 2 of our first mortgage loan investment secured by a luxury residential development located in Austin, Texas metropolitan area," said President, CEO & Director Albright. "Accordingly, net of the A-1 participation, our combined investment in Phase 1 and Phase 2 of this loan was $40 million at quarter end." He said the commercial loan portfolio totaled "$160.4 million" and that the loan mix reached "our targeted level of approximately 20% of our total undepreciated asset value."

  • "For the quarter, total revenue was $18.4 million, including lease income of $12.6 million and interest income from commercial loan investments of $5.8 million," said Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Philip Mays. "FFO and AFFO for the quarter were both $0.53 per diluted share."

Outlook

  • "We are raising our 2026 outlook for investment volume by $100 million," said President, CEO & Director Albright.

  • "For the full year 2026, we are increasing our FFO outlook to a new range of $2.09 to $2.13 per diluted share and our AFFO outlook to a new range of $2.11 to $2.15 per diluted share," said Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays. "Further...we are increasing our investment activity by $100 million to a new range of $170 million to $200 million." (In the prior quarter’s call, the company’s initial 2026 investment volume assumption was "$70 million to $100 million".)

  • "The guidance doesn't assume any incentive fee," said Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays.

Financial Results

  • "During the first quarter, we amended and restated our unsecured credit facility," said Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays. "Our new facility includes a $250 million revolver due February 2030...a $100 million term loan maturing in 2029 and a $100 million term loan maturing in 2031." He added, "with the closing of this facility, we now have no debt maturing for almost 3 years."

  • "Under our common ATM, we issued approximately 1.7 million shares...for net proceeds of $31.6 million," said Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays. "And under our preferred ATM...for net proceeds of $4.6 million." He said the quarter ended with "net debt to pro forma adjusted EBITDA of 6.6x and approximately $90 million of liquidity."

  • "The Board increased our quarterly common dividend by 5.3%...to $0.30 per share beginning this quarter," said Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays. "This new quarterly common dividend rate represents just a 57% AFFO payout ratio for the quarter."

Q&A

  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS Investment Bank: "can you just talk a little bit about...what's in the pipeline"; President, CEO & Director Albright said, "we have a fair amount of activity in the pipeline...trying to bring in some additional investment-grade credits" and that "as we have some lower-yielding loans burn off...That will be a nice recycle into higher-yielding and high-quality loans."

  • Jay Kornreich, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked if being near the 20% loan target implies "a shift in strategy" toward net lease; President, CEO & Director Albright said, "we do have a larger amount in the pipeline of traditional net lease investments," while Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays added, "we are near that 20% cap kind of an 80-20 blend."

  • Matthew Erdner, JonesTrading: asked about "loan to own" outcomes and lease rollover; President, CEO & Director Albright said, "most likely, none of these will turn into ownership positions" and on 2027-2028 rollover risk said, "feel very strong that these are going to be renewal candidates."

  • R.J. Milligan, Raymond James: asked whether any loans are on a watch list and about PIK collectability; Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays said, "We don't really have any credit concerns about any of the loans," and President, CEO & Director Albright added, "out of our loan book, there are no concerns right now."

  • Craig Kucera, Lucid Capital Markets: asked about cap-rate expectations; President, CEO & Director Albright said, "you're going to be closer to 7.5% this coming quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, concentrating on allocation limits (the 20% loan target), visibility into loan credit/PIK repayment, lease rollover/mark-to-market constraints, and acquisition pricing.

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, leaning on confidence language around pipeline and credit: "We feel very confident" (President, CEO & Director Albright) and "We don't really have any credit concerns" (Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays).

  • Versus the prior quarter, the tone shifted toward higher confidence on 2026 activity and capital deployment, supported by raised guidance and larger stated investment plans.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language turned more expansionary: Q1 2026 included "increasing" both earnings outlook ranges and lifting investment volume to "$170 million to $200 million," compared with Q4 2025’s initial assumptions of "$70 million to $100 million" investment volume and "$30 million to $60 million" disposition volume.

  • Strategic emphasis remained consistent on the property-plus-loans model, but Q1 2026 framed the loan portfolio as having reached the targeted scale: "now grown to our targeted level of approximately 20%."

  • Analysts continued probing loan allocation and credit visibility in both quarters; Q1 2026 also placed more emphasis on cap-rate trajectory and refinancing/ATM activity.

Risks and Concerns

  • Lease rollover upside appears constrained by option structures: "They have options. So unfortunately, it's going to be a set bump based on the renewal options," said President, CEO & Director Albright.

  • Funding cadence and earnings variability risk from loan payoffs was acknowledged: President, CEO & Director Albright said that as loans pay off, "there's something else in the pipeline" and described "lower-yielding loans" rolling off with planned reinvestment.

  • Loan underwriting conservatism and reserve mechanics were highlighted as mitigants: "we basically want to really have these loans structured pretty tightly," said President, CEO & Director Albright, while Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays described "pretty healthy reserves upfront" tied to items like taxes and interest.

Final Takeaway

Management presented Q1 2026 as a quarter of continued portfolio recycling and loan growth, highlighted by a $10 million Aspen acquisition, higher-yielding loan originations, and an expanded funding base via ATMs and a recast credit facility. The company raised full-year 2026 guidance ranges for both FFO and AFFO per diluted share and increased its targeted 2026 investment activity to $170 million-$200 million, while maintaining that the loan book has reached its intended ~20% scale and that the pipeline supports reinvestment as repayments occur.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
